context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:
Course context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on Problem 4.1 from the beaded string set. I’m especially stuck on how the wall boundary gets turned into something like \(A_1=-A_0\), and then how that leads to the allowed normal modes for the finite chain.
exchanges.tutor:
Good starting point! Let's focus on part (a) first.

Think about the left-hand wall itself: what physical condition does it impose on the displacement of the string at that boundary? What must the displacement be there, and where exactly is the wall located relative to the beads?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a broad opening question about Problem 4.1, covering parts (a), (b), and (c) all at once. Rather than scaffolding all three parts, I should focus on part (a) first and use the Socratic method to get them thinking about what the left-hand wall actually does physically. The key insight is that the wall enforces zero displacement at its location, and the 'image' bead A0 is introduced to mimic that constraint in an infinite system. I'll ask them to think about what physical condition the wall imposes, rather than explaining the method.

exchanges.student: I think the wall is fixed, so the displacement there has to be zero. The left wall is a distance \(a/2\) to the left of bead 1, so if bead 1 is at \(x=a/2\), then maybe the wall is at \(x=0\)?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right on both counts. Now, in the infinite-system trick, we introduce a fictitious bead A0 placed one spacing 'a' to the left of bead 1 — so at x = −a/2. The wall sits exactly halfway between A0 and bead 1.

If you assume the displacement varies sinusoidally across the beads, what value must A0 take so that the displacement is zero precisely at the wall's location (x = 0)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the zero-displacement condition at the wall and the geometry. Now I should push them to connect this physical condition to the mathematical trick of introducing an imaginary bead A0. The key step is: if we place a fictitious bead A0 at position x = -a/2 (one full spacing 'a' to the left of bead 1), and the wall is at x = 0 (the midpoint between A0 and A1), what condition on A0 ensures the displacement is zero at x = 0? I'll ask them to think about what value A0 must take to enforce zero displacement at the wall's location.

exchanges.student: I think that means the midpoint between \(A_0\) and \(A_1\) has to be zero, so \(\frac{A_0+A_1}{2}=0\). So that gives \(A_0=-A_1\), or equivalently \(A_1=-A_0\).
exchanges.tutor:
You've got the right result! One small thing to reflect on: is 'average the two amplitudes = 0' the most rigorous argument, or is there a cleaner symmetry reason why zero displacement at the midpoint forces A0 = −A1?

Either way, the conclusion is correct. Can you now apply the same logic to the **right-hand wall** for part (b)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the right answer but via a slightly hand-wavy argument (averaging the two amplitudes). The midpoint of the amplitudes being zero is not quite the same as the displacement at the midpoint being zero for a sinusoidal wave — it works here because the wall is exactly halfway between two beads, making the spatial interpolation symmetric, but the student should be aware this is a geometric symmetry argument rather than a simple average. However, this is close enough to the correct reasoning and the result is right. I should briefly affirm and gently nudge them to think about whether the averaging argument is rigorous or if there's a cleaner way to see it, then move them toward part (b).

exchanges.student: I’m guessing it should be similar: if the last real bead is \(A_N\), then I’d invent a fictitious bead \(A_{N+1}\) on the other side of the wall, and the wall is halfway between them. So I think the condition is \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\).
exchanges.tutor: Correct! Now for part (c), the real work begins. For a beaded string, what form do you expect the amplitude of bead $n$ to take in a normal mode? Think about what you know about sinusoidal solutions on a discrete chain.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the boundary condition for the right wall by symmetry with part (a). This is right. I should affirm and move them to part (c), which is the most substantial part — finding the normal modes and frequencies. The key is to use the assumed sinusoidal form for the bead amplitudes and apply both boundary conditions simultaneously. I'll ask them to think about what form the normal mode amplitudes take for a beaded string.

exchanges.student: I think I should try something like \(A_n = C e^{ikna}\), or equivalently a combination of sines and cosines. I’m not fully sure yet which form is easiest once the boundary conditions are applied.
exchanges.tutor: Good. Try applying the left boundary condition $A_1 = -A_0$ to your exponential form $A_n = Ce^{ikna}$. What equation does that give you for $k$, and does it suggest a simpler form for $A_n$?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea about the general form. I should nudge them to think about which form (complex exponential vs sine/cosine) is more natural given the boundary conditions they derived. The condition A0 = -A1 effectively means the solution must vanish at a half-step before bead 1, which is a shifted node condition. A sine form with the right argument will satisfy this naturally. I'll ask them to try applying their left boundary condition A1 = -A0 to the complex exponential form to see what constraint emerges.

exchanges.student: If I plug in \(A_1=Ce^{ika}\) and \(A_0=C\), then \(Ce^{ika}=-C\), so \(e^{ika}=-1\). That seems way too restrictive, so I think using only one exponential is wrong, and I probably need \(A_n=\alpha e^{ikna}+\beta e^{-ikna}\) instead.
exchanges.tutor: Good catch! Now apply $A_1 = -A_0$ to your two-term form $A_n = \alpha e^{ikna} + \beta e^{-ikna}$. What does that tell you about the relationship between $\alpha$ and $\beta$?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that a single exponential is too restrictive and that a general superposition is needed. This is good self-correction. Now I should guide them to apply the left boundary condition A0 = -A1 to this two-term form to find the relationship between α and β, which will naturally select the sine function.

exchanges.student:
Let me try it.

\(A_1=\alpha e^{ika}+\beta e^{-ika}\) and \(A_0=\alpha+\beta\), so the condition gives  
\[
\alpha e^{ika}+\beta e^{-ika}=-(\alpha+\beta).
\]
I’m a little stuck on simplifying that cleanly. Is this supposed to turn into a sine form centered at the wall?
exchanges.tutor: Good work setting it up. Try the specific guess $\beta = -\alpha$ and check: does that satisfy your equation? If so, what does $A_n$ simplify to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but struggling to see how the algebra collapses into a sine. Rather than explaining it, I should ask them to try a specific choice: what if they set β = -α? That's the natural choice that makes a sine, and I should let them verify it satisfies the boundary condition rather than just telling them.

exchanges.student:
If \(\beta=-\alpha\), then  
\[
A_n=\alpha(e^{ikna}-e^{-ikna})=2i\alpha \sin(kna),
\]
so that part looks nice. But then at \(n=0\), \(A_0=0\), and I’m confused because earlier we were using a nonzero fictitious \(A_0\) with \(A_1=-A_0\), so I feel like I set the origin wrong or picked the wrong sine form.
exchanges.tutor: Sharp observation! The issue is your choice of origin. Your sine vanishes at $n=0$, but the wall is not at $n=0$ — where is the wall located relative to your bead positions? Try placing the origin of your coordinate system at the wall itself and see what form $A_n$ takes.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a genuine and insightful confusion here. The issue is about where the node of the sine is located. With A_n = sin(kna), the node is at n=0, but the wall is not at n=0 — the wall is at a position halfway between n=0 and n=1, i.e., at x = a/2 if bead 1 is at x=a. The fix is to shift the argument so the sine vanishes at the wall location, not at n=0. The correct form should be A_n = sin(k(n - 1/2)a) or equivalently place the origin at the wall. I should guide the student to reconsider where to place the origin of their coordinate system — specifically, where should x=0 be so that the sine naturally vanishes there?

exchanges.student: Oh, right — the wall is halfway between the fictitious bead and bead 1, not at a bead position. So if I measure from the wall, I think bead \(n\) is at \(x=(n-\tfrac12)a\), and then maybe the mode shape should be \(A_n \propto \sin[k(n-\tfrac12)a]\). Can you check if that’s the right shifted form before I use the right boundary?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's the right form. Now apply your right-wall boundary condition $A_{N+1} = -A_N$ to it. What constraint does that place on $k$?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at the correct shifted sine form through good reasoning. I should affirm this is correct but not over-explain, and immediately push them to apply the right boundary condition to find the quantized values of k. That's the key remaining step for part (c).

exchanges.student:
I get
\[
A_N \propto \sin\!\big(k(N-\tfrac12)a\big),\qquad
A_{N+1} \propto \sin\!\big(k(N+\tfrac12)a\big).
\]
Then the boundary condition says
\[
\sin\!\big(k(N+\tfrac12)a\big)=-\sin\!\big(k(N-\tfrac12)a\big).
\]
I think that means \( \sin(kNa)\cos(ka/2)=0 \), but I’m not totally sure if I simplified that right.
exchanges.tutor: Your simplification is actually correct! So you have $\sin(kNa)\cos(ka/2) = 0$. This gives two families of solutions. What are the allowed values of $k$ from each factor, and can you think about which solutions are physically distinct normal modes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but made a small algebra error in the simplification. Using sum-to-product or product-to-sum identities: sin(k(N+1/2)a) + sin(k(N-1/2)a) = 2sin(kNa)cos(ka/2) = 0. That's correct! So the student actually got it right. I should confirm the algebra is correct and ask them what the two cases (sin(kNa)=0 or cos(ka/2)=0) give for allowed k values, and which one is physically relevant.
